L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was on his last day in Lauterbrunnen and described as 'desperate' to get one final jump before his flight home the next morning. This time pressure and reluctance to waste the trip created misplaced motivation to proceed despite clearly dangerous conditions, directly contributing to his decision to jump when two partners walked down and explicitly warned him.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Despite observable evidence that conditions were too dangerous (horizontal flags, sustained gusts, 45 minutes of worsening conditions, two experienced partners declining), the jumper believed he could safely complete the jump. His dismissal of explicit warnings ('he didn't care') indicates he overestimated his ability or his canopy's capability to handle the wind conditions.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper packed in a rush the evening before because he wanted to go to sleep early to catch early morning conditions. This haste during packing contributed to the technique error of misrouting the bridle around the leg strap, a rigging mistake that would likely have been caught with careful, unhurried packing.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention/Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: Neither the jumper nor his partner performed a gear check at the exit point despite standing right next to each other. The narrative describes this as a straightforward omission rather than time pressure or distraction at the exit, suggesting a failure to maintain sufficient alertness to standard pre-jump procedures. This inattention enabled the memory failure of skipping the gear check that would have caught the misrouted bridle.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's immediate pre-jump preparation was inadequate: he did not trace the bridle routing before exiting, nor did his partner. The narrative explicitly states that running a hand along the bridle would have immediately detected the misrouting. This preparatory failure directly enabled the memory-lapse unsafe act by allowing the packing error to persist undetected to the point of exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: Heavy fresh snow with no shadows, no contrast, and no texture created complete flat light conditions that eliminated all visual depth cues. The jumper's sensory systems failed to correctly perceive his altitude above the snowfield, causing him to believe he had plenty of altitude when he was at ground level. This perceptual degradation directly produced the Layer 1 visual illusion / perceptual error.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate site assessment for flat light
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper flew a wingsuit proximity line over a featureless snowfield on a day with heavy fresh snow, low-level clouds, no shadows, and no contrast. Adequate pre-jump site assessment should have identified that these flat light conditions would eliminate the depth cues necessary for altitude awareness during proximity flight over snow terrain. This preparation failure created the conditions under which the perceptual error occurred.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: After all four experienced companions unanimously stood down due to dangerous wind conditions, the jumper chose to remain at the exit and wait, then launched alone. This decision to persist after the entire group had called the conditions unsafe suggests misplaced motivation—reluctance to abandon the effort after spending the morning at the exit, or a drive to jump that overrode the clear social and environmental signals to abort.

L2_preconditions.label: Strong crosswind gust on exit
L2_preconditions.description: The strong crosswind gust that caught the jumper immediately upon exit pushed him sideways and back toward the wall, destabilizing him from stable to head-low within the first second. This environmental force directly degraded his ability to maintain a stable exit and reach his pilot chute, contributing to the technique error of failing to deploy during the ensuing tumble along the cliff face.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was severely hungover from heavy drinking until 4am the night before. The narrative explicitly states the hangover affected his coordination, directly contributing to his legs buckling on the exit push-off and the resulting unstable body position.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper fixated entirely on recovering stability during the approximately 6 seconds of freefall, to the exclusion of the critical task of deploying the pilot chute. This narrowing of attention on body position recovery prevented him from recognizing that deployment—even from an unstable position—was the priority action needed to survive.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's severely degraded physiological state from heavy alcohol consumption the night before likely impaired his cognitive processing and decision-making capacity during the brief freefall window, contributing to his failure to prioritize deployment over stability recovery.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states that the jumper had jumped hungover before without issues, indicating a pattern of jumping while physiologically impaired. This normalization of jumping in a degraded state was a sustained failure to correct a known risky behavior, which on this occasion contributed to the exit technique failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Despite being visibly unwell—splitting headache, vomiting twice on the trail, visibly unsteady—and despite peers advising him to sit out, the jumper pressed forward with the jump, stating adrenaline would compensate. His motivation to proceed was driven by factors other than a sound assessment of his readiness.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient tracking suit experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only two skydives in the tracking suit and zero cliff jumps with it, far below the accepted minimum of 40-50 tracking suit skydives before BASE use. This lack of training and familiarity with the suit directly caused his inability to achieve an effective tracking position and forward drive during the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Physical Overexertion
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper drove 14 hours straight through the night and was visibly exhausted at the exit point. This acute physical exhaustion from sleep deprivation and prolonged driving degraded his physical and cognitive capacity, impairing his ability to execute the demanding body position required to fly the tracking suit effectively.

L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper drove through the night without sleep and was visibly exhausted. This fatigue degraded his judgment and decision-making capacity, contributing to his decision to proceed with an unfamiliar tracking suit on a demanding cliff despite inadequate experience with the equipment.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper dismissed a more experienced jumper's suggestion to start with a more forgiving exit, stating the chosen exit 'looked easy from the footage.' This indicates he overestimated his ability to fly the tracking suit based on watching videos rather than actual experience, misjudging his capability relative to the task demands.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper drove 14 hours straight to reach the site, creating psychological investment in completing the jump. This sunk-cost commitment likely contributed to his reluctance to accept the mentor's suggestion to choose a more forgiving exit, pressing forward despite inadequate preparation with the tracking suit.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment familiarization before jump
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper purchased the tracking suit only a month prior and completed only two skydives in it, never jumping it from a cliff. He failed to adequately familiarize himself with the suit's flight characteristics through sufficient skydiving practice before attempting to use it on a slider-up cliff requiring mandatory sub-terminal tracking.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient training in off-heading correction techniques
L2_preconditions.description: With 90 BASE jumps over 3 seasons, the jumper's reaction to a 180° off-heading — grabbing a single toggle and pulling hard rather than using rear risers or both toggles to first arrest forward speed — indicates insufficient training and practice in off-heading correction procedures. Accepted practice requires extensive drilling of heading corrections at simple objects (spans) so that the correct response becomes automatic muscle memory. The jumper's technique error suggests this critical skill had not been adequately developed.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Upon opening with a 180° off-heading facing the building, the jumper went straight for her toggles and, finding only the right one, pulled it hard without transitioning to rear risers or attempting to locate the left toggle. This suggests channelized attention on the first control input found rather than assessing the full situation and selecting the correct correction technique. The fixation on the single available toggle excluded consideration of alternative and more appropriate responses such as rear riser input.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention/Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had 10 BASE seasons and stated he had done this exit dozens of times and 'knew the heading by feel.' This high familiarity with the site produced an unjustified reduction in alertness to the actual hazards present, leading him to believe he could safely navigate without visual reference while all other jumpers recognized the conditions as unjumpable.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper explicitly stated he could track on the right bearing and clear everything by feel alone, despite zero visibility. This represents an unreasonable overestimation of his own capability to navigate terrain without visual reference. His self-assessment was inconsistent with the actual demands of the task — no amount of experience enables safe terrain clearance when protruding features are invisible.

L2_preconditions.label: Zero visibility fog during freefall
L2_preconditions.description: The fog that obscured the wall, fjord, and landing area directly degraded the jumper's ability to perceive terrain proximity during freefall and tracking. Without visual reference, he could not detect the protruding ledge, could not correct his flight path, and could not recognize the need to deploy. The fog was the environmental condition that made the L1 technique error (tracking into terrain with PC still stowed) unrecoverable. This is coded as a precondition to the 102S act, distinct from the 101D decision to jump in those conditions.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Marcus became fixated on adjusting his helmet-mounted GoPro during flight, channeling his attention onto the camera equipment to the exclusion of monitoring terrain proximity, body position, and object separation. This narrowing of attention directly enabled the skill-based attention failure identified at Layer 1.

L2_preconditions.label: Distraction
L2_preconditions.description: The helmet-mounted GoPro served as an external stimulus that diverted Marcus's attention from the primary task of tracking away from the wall. The physical act of reaching up to adjust the camera interrupted his tracking body position and removed his focus from terrain awareness at a critical moment.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Marcus was designated as the filmer on this two-way jump. The motivation to capture video of the lead jumper likely drove his decision to adjust the camera mid-flight rather than prioritize separation and tracking. This filming role created a competing priority that contributed to the attention failure.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper experienced at least four near-miss stalls, explicitly acknowledged his angle of attack control was inadequate, stated he should return to the dropzone for 50 skydives to work on stall recovery, and never did so. This sustained pattern of knowing about a serious deficiency and failing to take corrective action directly enabled his continued decision to jump cliffs with an unresolved skill deficit.

L2_preconditions.label: Normalization of Deviance
L2_preconditions.description: Despite four near-miss stalls each following the same pattern, the jumper continued jumping cliffs every weekend, gradually accepting repeated near-fatal events as tolerable rather than treating them as urgent signals to stop and retrain. This progressive normalization of increasingly dangerous outcomes reinforced his decision to keep flying near terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient AoA control skill for proximity flying
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's angle of attack control was self-admittedly inadequate for the demands of wingsuit proximity flying. The same technique failure—allowing AoA to get too high, entering a stall—had occurred on at least four prior occasions, demonstrating that his skill level was insufficient for the task. This training deficit was the direct basis for the recurring stall pattern that proved fatal on this jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: Multiple witnesses stated the jumper 'completely froze the moment he saw the wires directly in front of him,' with arms remaining at his sides and no corrective input attempted. This automatic physiological freeze response triggered by the unexpected sight of guy wires directly ahead rendered him unable to initiate any heading correction within the approximately two-second window available.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient antenna experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 60 BASE jumps and just two prior antenna jumps, both from taller structures with guy wires further from the exit point. This limited antenna experience — particularly the lack of experience with guy wires in close proximity — likely contributed to his inability to execute a trained response under the sudden stress of an off-heading opening toward nearby wires. Accepted progression norms emphasize extensive practice of off-heading corrections at forgiving objects before progressing to objects where off-headings are immediately life-threatening.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate site assessment for experience level
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's two previous antenna jumps were from taller structures with guy wires further from the exit point. Jumping a 900-foot antenna at 800 feet — where guy wires were approximately 40 feet from the opening point — represented a significantly more constrained heading environment than he had previously experienced. The failure to adequately assess how the closer guy wire proximity changed the demands of this jump contributed to his being in a situation where a 90-degree off-heading was immediately fatal.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jump was conducted at night (23:30), which is consistent with illegal antenna jumping where timing is driven by avoiding detection rather than optimal conditions. The illegality and nighttime execution add stress and complication that degrade performance, and the decision to proceed at night at an unfamiliar antenna type suggests motivation driven by factors other than a sound assessment of readiness and conditions.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper examined the torn PC, was advised by multiple people to replace it, was offered spare PCs, and still concluded it would 'catch enough air.' This demonstrates an unreasonable overestimation of the damaged equipment's capability despite clear evidence and peer feedback to the contrary, enabling the decision to jump with known-defective gear.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate multi-way briefing and separation plan
L2_preconditions.description: The group did not establish exit order separation delays, flight paths, or opening altitudes. This lack of a coordinated separation plan created the conditions under which David tracked directly over the second jumper without adequate vertical or horizontal separation, making his failure to check airspace before deployment consequential.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention/Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: David failed to maintain situational awareness of other jumpers in his airspace during a multi-way jump. Despite tracking directly over the second jumper, he did not check below before deploying, indicating a failure to maintain the attention required for safe deployment in proximity to other jumpers.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was aware of his progressively lower pulls through feedback from other jumpers who spoke to him about it multiple times. Despite this explicit external warning, he failed to take corrective action and continued extending his delays from 4 to 6 to 8+ seconds over the preceding season. This sustained pattern of inaction in the face of known risk directly enabled the fatal decision to pull one second too late.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's progressive extension of delays from 4 to 8+ seconds over a single season, despite repeated warnings from peers, indicates he unreasonably overestimated his ability to judge minimum deployment altitude. Each successful low pull reinforced his belief that he could push further, creating a self-assessment inconsistent with the actual margin available on a span jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's motivation to progressively extend delays was driven by factors other than a sound assessment of readiness and safe margins. The pattern of pushing delays longer and longer suggests a performance-driven or thrill-seeking motivation that overrode prudent judgment about minimum safe deployment altitude.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient wingsuit-specific experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only approximately 10 skydives in the Aura 1 expert wingsuit, far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 skydives with the specific suit before wingsuit BASE jumping. Expert-level wingsuits have stall boundaries that require many flights to understand. His insufficient familiarity with the suit's flight envelope directly contributed to his inability to manage AoA and recover from the stall during flight.

L2_preconditions.label: Premature progression to expert wingsuit
L2_preconditions.description: With approximately 40 wingsuit BASE jumps using intermediate suits and only about 10 skydives in the Aura 1, the jumper's overall experience level was insufficient for an expert-level wingsuit in BASE. Accepted progression norms require at least 100 wingsuit skydives including 40-60 with the specific suit before wingsuit BASE. His 200 total wingsuit skydives met the overall minimum, but his near-total lack of time in the expert suit meant he lacked the skill foundation needed to make a sound judgment about using it off a cliff.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper chose to fly an expert-level wingsuit off a cliff after only ~10 skydives in it, indicating he overestimated his ability to safely manage the suit's flight characteristics in a BASE environment. The narrative explicitly states the suit has stall boundaries requiring many flights to understand and that he had not put in that time, yet he proceeded to fly a proximity line, demonstrating a misjudgment of his capability relative to the task demands.

L2_preconditions.label: Thermal activity during flight
L2_preconditions.description: The weather was described as sunny and hot with thermals producing. Thermal activity can create unpredictable lift and sink zones along a flight path, potentially contributing to the AoA shift and stall by altering the air mass the jumper was flying through. While the primary cause of the stall was the jumper's technique, active thermals during a hot afternoon in Chamonix could have degraded his ability to maintain stable flight, particularly given his unfamiliarity with the suit's stall boundaries.

L2_preconditions.label: Grossly insufficient training and experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 5 skydives, zero BASE jumps, zero wingsuit skydives, and no reported BASE training or mentorship. This is massively below the accepted minimum of 200+ skydives before beginning BASE, and represents a complete absence of the foundational skills (freefall body control, canopy piloting, tracking) needed to evaluate whether a slider-up cliff jump was within their capability. This lack of experience directly enabled the catastrophically poor decision to attempt the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: No freefall or tracking competence
L2_preconditions.description: With only 5 skydives total and no BASE jumps, the jumper had no meaningful freefall body position skills, no sub-terminal tracking ability, and no experience with BASE deployment sequences. Accepted progression requires extensive skydiving, tracking suit proficiency built over 40-50+ tracking suit skydives, and progression through simple BASE objects before attempting slider-up cliffs. The complete absence of these skills made it impossible for the jumper to execute a stable exit, effective track, or properly timed deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper attempted a slider-up tracking suit cliff jump with 5 skydives and zero BASE experience, indicating a gross overestimation of their own capability relative to the demands of the task. The gap between the jumper's actual ability and the minimum competence required for this jump type is so extreme that proceeding implies the jumper fundamentally misjudged what was required or believed they could perform far beyond their actual skill level.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention/Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had been going back and forth on whether to jump or hike down due to deteriorating weather. This indecision and the stress of the weather situation likely diverted mental attention away from the packing task, contributing to the critical omission of failing to route the PC to the BOC pouch. The error — placing the PC inside the container rather than routing it out — is a fundamental procedural step that a jumper with 90 BASE jumps would normally perform correctly, suggesting degraded attention during packing rather than a knowledge deficit.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative describes deteriorating weather with increasing wind and rain, and the jumper going back and forth on whether to jump or hike down. Being the last one at the exit while conditions worsened creates pressure to either commit quickly or abandon. This pressure to resolve the decision — combined with worsening conditions that may have rushed the packing process — contributed to the procedural omission during packing.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: A pre-jump gear check or trace of the deployment system from BOC handle through bridle to PC would have revealed that the PC was not in the BOC pouch but was instead trapped inside the container. The failure to perform this verification before jumping allowed the packing error to go undetected and directly enabled the total malfunction.
